revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Sendai City Board of Education, ‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’ (Sendai City Cultural Property Survey Report No. 9), March 1976.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ''' [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000065.html Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group]

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Sendai City Board of Education, ‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’ (Sendai City Cultural Property Survey Report No. 9), March 1976.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ''' [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000065.html Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group]
[[Category:Ancient tombs in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Clustered tombs]] [[Category:Rock-cut tombs]] [[ja:宗禅寺横穴墓群]]

3 tombs * {{ill|Takinojō Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝之城横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Tokorozawa|en}}[[ja:所沢市]], Saitama) - 9 tombs * {{ill|Kitaakitsu Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:北秋津横穴墓群]] (Tokorozawa, Saitama) - 8 tombs * {{ill|Bikuniyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:比丘尼山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Higashimatsuyama|en}}[[ja:東松山市]], Saitama) - Over 50 tombs * {{ill|Takane Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:高根横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kumagaya|en}}[[ja:熊谷市]], Saitama) - Municipal Historic Site * {{ill|Nagara Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:長柄横穴群]] ({{ill|Nagara, Chiba|en}}[[ja:長柄町]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 300 tombs * {{ill|Deyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:出山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Mitaka, Tokyo|en}}[[ja:三鷹市]], [[Tokyo]]) - Metropolitan Historic Site, 10 tombs * {{ill|Nakawada Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:中和田横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Tama, Tokyo|en}}[[ja:多摩市]], Tokyo) - 14 tombs * {{ill|Todoroki Ravine Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:谷沢川横穴群]] ({{ill|Setagaya|en}}[[ja:世田谷区]], Tokyo) * {{ill|Ekoda Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:荏子田横穴]] ({{ill|Aoba-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:青葉区]], {{ill|Yokohama|en}}[[ja:横浜市]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]) - Municipal Historic Site, 2 tombs * {{ill|Ichigao Cave Tomb Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴古墳群]] (Aoba-ku, Yokohama) - Prefectural Historic Site, 19 tombs * {{ill|Itachigawa Basin Rock-Cut Tombs|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群|en}}[[ja:㹨川流域横穴墓群]] (Kamakura-style tomb concentration) ** {{ill|Matsugasaki Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:松ヶ崎横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kōnan-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:港南区]], Yokohama) - Municipal Historic Site, 8 tombs ** {{ill|Shichisekiyama Cave Tomb Cluster|ja|七石山横穴墓群|en}}[[ja:七石山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Sakae-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:栄区]], Yokohama) - Over 100 tombs originally ** {{ill|Miyanomae Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:宮ノ前横穴墓群]] (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 23 tombs ** {{ill|Hikoshigaya Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:ひこしヶ谷横穴墓群]] (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 12 tombs
=== Tokai Region === * {{ill|Kashiya Cave Tombs|ja|柏谷横穴群|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] ({{ill|Kannami|en}}[[ja:函南町]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 106 tombs * {{ill|Kitaema Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:北江間横穴群]] ({{ill|Izunokuni|en}}[[ja:伊豆の国市]], Shizuoka) - National Historic Site, 101 tombs * {{ill|E-no-ura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:江浦横穴群]] ({{ill|Numazu|en}}[[ja:沼津市]], Shizuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, 92 tombs
=== Hokuriku Region === * {{ill|Hōōzan Cave Tombs||ja|法皇山横穴古墳|en}}[[ja:法皇山横穴古墳]] ({{ill|Kaga, Ishikawa|en}}[[ja:加賀市]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 150 tombs
=== Kinki Region === * {{ill|Takaida Cave Tombs|en}}[[ja:高井田横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|en}}[[ja:柏原市]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Anfukuji Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:安福寺横穴群]] (Kashiwara, Osaka) - 32 tombs
=== Chūgoku Region === * {{ill|Jūōmen Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:十王免横穴群]] ({{ill|Matsue|en}}[[ja:松江市]], [[Shimane Prefecture]]) - 37 tombs * {{ill|Kitsunedani Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:狐谷横穴墓群]] (Matsue, Shimane) - 16 tombs * {{ill|Kamikonishi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:上塩冶横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Izumo, Shimane|en}}[[ja:出雲市]], Shimane) * {{ill|Takatsuku Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:高津久横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Chibu, Shimane|en}}[[ja:知夫村]], [[Oki District]])
=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Cave Tomb|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Cave Tomb Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasugaike Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site

This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}

== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ''' [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000065.html Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group]

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Sendai City Board of Education, ‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’ (Sendai City Cultural Property Survey Report No. 9), March 1976.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ''' [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000065.html Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group]
[[Category:Ancient tombs in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Clustered tombs]] [[Category:Rock-cut tombs]] [[ja:宗禅寺横穴墓群]]

14 tombs * {{ill|Todoroki Ravine Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:谷沢川横穴群]] ({{ill|Setagaya|en}}[[ja:世田谷区]], Tokyo) * {{ill|Ekoda Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:荏子田横穴]] ({{ill|Aoba-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:青葉区]], {{ill|Yokohama|en}}[[ja:横浜市]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]) - Municipal Historic Site, 2 tombs * {{ill|Ichigao Cave Tomb Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:市ヶ尾横穴古墳群]] (Aoba-ku, Yokohama) - Prefectural Historic Site, 19 tombs * {{ill|Itachigawa Basin Rock-Cut Tombs|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群|en}}[[ja:㹨川流域横穴墓群]] (Kamakura-style tomb concentration) ** {{ill|Matsugasaki Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:松ヶ崎横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kōnan-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:港南区]], Yokohama) - Municipal Historic Site, 8 tombs ** {{ill|Shichisekiyama Cave Tomb Cluster|ja|七石山横穴墓群|en}}[[ja:七石山横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Sakae-ku, Yokohama|en}}[[ja:栄区]], Yokohama) - Over 100 tombs originally ** {{ill|Miyanomae Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:宮ノ前横穴墓群]] (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 23 tombs ** {{ill|Hikoshigaya Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:ひこしヶ谷横穴墓群]] (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 12 tombs
=== Tokai Region === * {{ill|Kashiya Cave Tombs|ja|柏谷横穴群|en}}[[ja:柏谷横穴群]] ({{ill|Kannami|en}}[[ja:函南町]], [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 106 tombs * {{ill|Kitaema Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:北江間横穴群]] ({{ill|Izunokuni|en}}[[ja:伊豆の国市]], Shizuoka) - National Historic Site, 101 tombs * {{ill|E-no-ura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:江浦横穴群]] ({{ill|Numazu|en}}[[ja:沼津市]], Shizuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, 92 tombs
=== Hokuriku Region === * {{ill|Hōōzan Cave Tombs||ja|法皇山横穴古墳|en}}[[ja:法皇山横穴古墳]] ({{ill|Kaga, Ishikawa|en}}[[ja:加賀市]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 150 tombs
=== Kinki Region === * {{ill|Takaida Cave Tombs|en}}[[ja:高井田横穴墓群]] ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|en}}[[ja:柏原市]], [[Osaka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Anfukuji Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:安福寺横穴群]] (Kashiwara, Osaka) - 32 tombs

=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en}}[[ja:竹並遺跡]] ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}[[ja:行橋市]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:城山横穴群]] ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:福智町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en}}[[ja:水町遺跡]] ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}[[ja:直方市]], Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Cave Tomb|en}}[[ja:古月横穴]] ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}[[ja:鞍手町]], Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫ナギノ横穴群]] ({{ill|Tamana|en}}[[ja:玉名市]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:石貫穴観音横穴群]] (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:鍋田横穴群]] ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en}}[[ja:山鹿市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:大村横穴群]] ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en}}[[ja:人吉市]], Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}}[[ja:四日市横穴群]] ({{ill|Usa|en}}[[ja:宇佐市]], [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Cave Tomb Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:滝尾百穴横穴古墳群]] ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en}}[[ja:大分市]], Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasugaike Kofun Cluster|en}}[[ja:蓮ヶ池横穴群]] ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en}}[[ja:宮崎市]], [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site

'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.

Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.

In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:kkkk]]" to ""
revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Sendai City Board of Education, ‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’ (Sendai City Cultural Property Survey Report No. 9), March 1976.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ''' [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000065.html Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Corridor-type kofun forks]] to [[Category:Cave tombs]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Yokouana Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Yokouana Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Yokouana Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Sendai City Board of Education, ‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Yokouana Group Excavation Report’ (Sendai City Cultural Property Survey Report No. 9), March 1976.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ''' [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000065.html Sōzenji Yokouana Tomb Group]

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.

From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs.
