revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}, [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}, Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}, Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Cave Tomb|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}, Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Tamana|en}}, [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Usa|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Cave Tomb Kofun Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasugaike Kofun Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site

revision.comment: 13 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:宗禅寺横穴墓群 up to rev 104221047
revision.text:
{{Location map|Japan Miyagi Prefecture|relief=1| label = 宗禅寺
横穴墓群| position = bottom| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 250| caption ={{Center|位置}}}} '''宗禅寺横穴墓群'''（そうぜんじよこあなぼぐん）は、[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[太白区]]根岸町にある[[横穴墓群]]である。'''宗禅寺横穴群'''（そうぜんじよこあなぐん）とも呼ばれる。7世紀後半から8世紀後半にかけて、[[広瀬川 (宮城県)|広瀬川]]の右岸（南西岸）に造営された。総数は不明だが、発見された横穴墓は15基ある。 ==立地== [[広瀬川 (宮城県)|広瀬川]]右岸で丘陵から平地に変わる一帯には、[[愛宕山横穴墓群]]、[[大年寺山横穴墓群]]などがあり、宗禅寺横穴墓群とともに[[向山横穴墓群]]をなす。宗禅寺横穴墓群は、[[大年寺山]]の尾根の先を広瀬川がかすめる位置、[[宗禅寺]]の墓地にある。現在は山裾と広瀬川の河原の間に川から比高10メートルほどの平らな[[段丘]]があるが、古代には大年寺山の[[尾根]]がもう少し伸びて小さな谷をなしていた。 その谷の傾斜を横から掘りこんで墓としたのが、宗禅寺横穴墓群である。東西に並ぶようにして知られる限り[[横穴墓]]は15基確認されている。後に谷は[[土砂]]で埋もれ、2、3メートルほどの崖として残り、横穴も岸から離れた一部を除いて地面の下になった。[[発掘調査]]は埋没谷の深部まで掘り下げずに終えたので、下のほうにまだ知られていない横穴が存在する可能性がある『仙台市根岸町宗禅寺横穴群発掘調査報告書』8頁。。 遺跡の南東には[[724年]]まで[[陸奥国]]の[[国府]]があったとされる[[郡山遺跡]]があり、時期的に横穴墓の使用期間と重なる。広瀬川の対岸、東方には[[古墳時代]]から続く集落である[[南小泉遺跡]]がある。これらが横穴墓の利用者の居住地の候補である。 ==横穴墓の概要== 発掘担当者によって、15の横穴墓は西から東へ、山に近いほうから川に近いほうへ、1号墳から15号墳と番号を振られた。[[1953年]]（昭和28年）に見つかった穴が15号墳である。どの墓も崖面に対して直角にまっすぐ入り、崖がのびる方向に従い、西の端では南を、東の端では東南を向く。7、8、9号墳を除けばほぼ同じ高さにそろう。 横穴の奥に[[玄室]]があり、断面形状は方形・[[長方形]]・[[台形]]で、床の1辺は1.4メートルから2.75メートルにおさまる。玄室の上は[[アーチ]]状であったり家の屋根の形であったりする。入り口（玄門）は、そこに至るトンネル（[[羨道]]）より幅を狭く作る。13号墳だけは、幅0.8メートルで、玄室・玄門・羨道の区別がないタイプである。11号墳と12号墳は玄室が接触して数十センチの穴が通じていた。1号墳、2号墳、3号墳も玄室が横でつながっていたが、これは[[ピッケル]]のような[[工具]]で穴を開けたらしく、後世の改変と考えられる。 7つの横穴では、約10センチメートルほど高くした台床が作られた。そのうち15号墳だけは高さが約50センチメートルで、顕著な壇をなした。台床がない穴は8である。岩には水が滲み出る自然の割れ目が走っていたため、横穴の中にはこれにあわせて排水用の溝を設けたものがある。台床の上で、5号墳から1体分、9号墳から3体分にあたると推測される人骨が見つかった。泥質化が著しく、見つかったのは頭蓋骨など大きな骨が少数で、状態はよくなかった。5号墳のものは熟年男性、9号墳のものは熟年男性2人と壮年女性1人と推定された。 遺物としては、7つの横穴から[[須恵器]]13点、[[土師器]]8点、鉄製[[刀子]]1点が見つかった。須恵器・土師器の発見場所は、玄室内、玄門閉塞部の外、穴の外の前庭部の3箇所である。それぞれ、遺体とともに副葬したもの、葬儀の後に供献したもの、追葬のために準備して置いたものに対応すると考えられる。刀子は約15.6センチで、木の柄がついており、5号墳の台床の上、つまり人骨と同じ場所にあった。 ==その後の歴史== 横穴の利用が絶えて久しくなった中世に、この低い崖から少し北に離れて[[宗禅寺 (仙台市)|宗禅寺]]が開山した。遺跡がある崖は宗禅寺の所有地となり、崖上と崖下が両方とも寺の墓地になった『仙台市根岸町宗禅寺横穴群発掘調査報告書』4頁。。4号墳では、近世以後に作られた墓穴の床が横穴の天井に接近して作られ、崩れて30センチほどの穴が発掘調査時に開いていた。 穴の存在は寺にも近所の人にも知られており、浮浪者が住みついたこともあった。時代が不明だがおそらく居住者が拡張のために3つの横穴を内部で連結した。昭和の初めに、安全のためなどの理由で、付近の住民が地表に出てくる穴の入り口を土で埋めて閉塞した。 [[1953年]]、[[宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|宮沢橋]]架け替えに伴い広瀬川に沿う道路を造る工事の際、削った崖の中から横穴が一つ見つかった。[[1970年代]]に、もう一段岸から離れたところに[[広瀬河畔通り]]を通すことになり、宗禅寺の墓地の移転が必要になった。横穴があった崖が移転先に含まれることとなり、仙台市教育委員会と仙台市建設局土木部が[[1975年]]（昭和50年）1月13日から2月15日にかけて約300m2の発掘調査を実施した。既知のものと併せて14の横穴を確認、調査した。調査後、[[整地]]のために一部が破壊され、大部分が土中に埋められた。遺跡は現在は宗禅寺の墓地の下にあり、横穴の存在をうかがわせるものは何もない。 ==　脚注 == {{reflist}} ==参考文献== *仙台市教育委員会『仙台市根岸町宗禅寺横穴群発掘調査報告書』（仙台市文化財調査報告書第9集）、1976年3月。 == 外部リンク == * [https://www.city.sendai.jp/chosachose/kurashi/manabu/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazai/iseki.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [https://www.sendai-c.ed.jp/~bunkazai/isekidb/c00089.html 宗禅寺横穴墓群] {{Coord|38|14|30.4|N|140|53|01.0|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=title}} {{考古学}} {{日本の文化財}} {{DEFAULTSORT:そうせんしよこあなくん}} [[Category:宮城県の古墳]] [[Category:群集墳]] [[Category:横穴墓]]

(広瀬川)|宮沢橋]]架け替えに伴い広瀬川に沿う道路を造る工事の際、削った崖の中から横穴が一つ見つかった。[[1970年代]]に、もう一段岸から離れたところに[[広瀬河畔通り]]を通すことになり、宗禅寺の墓地の移転が必要になった。横穴があった崖が移転先に含まれることとなり、仙台市教育委員会と仙台市建設局土木部が[[1975年]]（昭和50年）1月13日から2月15日にかけて約300m2の発掘調査を実施した。既知のものと併せて14の横穴を確認、調査した。調査後、[[整地]]のために一部が破壊され、大部分が土中に埋められた。遺跡は現在は宗禅寺の墓地の下にあり、横穴の存在をうかがわせるものは何もない。 ==　脚注 == {{reflist}} ==参考文献== *仙台市教育委員会『仙台市根岸町宗禅寺横穴群発掘調査報告書』（仙台市文化財調査報告書第9集）、1976年3月。 == 外部リンク == * [https://www.city.sendai.jp/chosachose/kurashi/manabu/kyoiku/inkai/bunkazai/bunkazai/iseki.html 仙台市の文化財]（[[仙台市教育委員会]]） ** [https://www.sendai-c.ed.jp/~bunkazai/isekidb/c00089.html 宗禅寺横穴墓群] {{Coord|38|14|30.4|N|140|53|01.0|E|region:JP-04_type:landmark|display=title}} {{考古学}} {{日本の文化財}} {{DEFAULTSORT:そうせんしよこあなくん}} [[Category:宮城県の古墳]] [[Category:群集墳]] [[Category:横穴墓]]

revision.comment: Bot: restore translated version
revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Sendai City Board of Education, ‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’ (Sendai City Cultural Property Survey Report No. 9), March 1976.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ''' [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000065.html Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group]
== Distribution and Notable Cave Tomb Tombsnger extant == * {{ill|Sōzenji Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} (Taihaku-ku, Sendai) * {{ill|Kiyotosaku Cave Tomb|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Futaba, Fukushima|en}}, [[Fukushima Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 300 tombs * {{ill|Izumizaki Cave Tomb|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Nishishirakawa District|en}}, {{ill|Izumizaki, Fukushima|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nakata Cave Tombs|ja|中田横穴|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|en}}) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Hayama Cave Tomb|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Minamisōma|en}}, Fukushima Prefecture) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Wada Daibutsu and Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Sukagawa|en}}, Fukushima Prefecture) - Municipal Historic Site
=== Kantō Region === * {{ill|Jūgorō-ana Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Hitachinaka, Ibaraki|en}}, [[Ibaraki Prefecture]]) - Over 300 tombs * {{ill|Karanogosho Cave Tomb|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Nasukaragawa, Tochigi|en}}, [[Tochigi Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, part of the Hokōda and Wami Cave Tomb Clusters * {{ill|Nagaoka Hundred Caves|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Utsunomiya|en}}, Tochigi Prefecture) - 52 tombs * {{ill|Yoshimi Hundred Caves|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Yoshimi, Saitama|en}}, [[Saitama Prefecture]]) - 3 tombs * {{ill|Takinojō Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Tokorozawa|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Saitama) - 9 tombs * {{ill|Kitaakitsu Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} (Tokorozawa, Saitama) - 8 tombs * {{ill|Bikuniyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Higashimatsuyama|en}}, Saitama) - Over 50 tombs * {{ill|Takane Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kumagaya|en}}, Saitama) - Municipal Historic Site * {{ill|Nagara Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Nagara, Chiba|en}}, [[Chiba Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 300 tombs * {{ill|Deyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Mitaka, Tokyo|en}}, [[Tokyo]]) - Metropolitan Historic Site, 10 tombs * {{ill|Nakawada Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Tama, Tokyo|en}}, Tokyo) - 14 tombs * {{ill|Todoroki Ravine Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Setagaya|en}}, Tokyo) * {{ill|Ekoda Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Aoba-ku, Yokohama|en}}, {{ill|Yokohama|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]) - Municipal Historic Site, 2 tombs * {{ill|Ichigao Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} (Aoba-ku, Yokohama) - Prefectural Historic Site, 19 tombs * {{ill|Itachigawa Basin Rock-Cut Tombs|ja|㹨川流域横穴墓群|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kamakura-style tomb concentration) ** {{ill|Matsugasaki Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Kōnan-ku, Yokohama|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Yokohama) - Municipal Historic Site, 8 tombs ** {{ill|Shichisekiyama Cave Tomb Cluster|ja|七石山横穴墓群|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Sakae-ku, Yokohama|en}}, Yokohama) - Over 100 tombs originally ** {{ill|Miyanomae Tunnel Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 23 tombs ** {{ill|Hikoshigaya Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} (Sakae-ku, Yokohama) - 12 tombs
=== Tokai Region === * {{ill|Kashiya Cave Tombs|ja|柏谷横穴群|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kannami|en}}, [[Shizuoka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 106 tombs * {{ill|Kitaema Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Izunokuni|en}}, Shizuoka) - National Historic Site, 101 tombs * {{ill|E-no-ura Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Numazu|en}}, Shizuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, 92 tombs
=== Hokuriku Region === * {{ill|Hōōzan Cave Tombs||ja|法皇山横穴古墳|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kaga, Ishikawa|en}}, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 150 tombs
=== Kinki Region === * {{ill|Takaida Cave Tombs|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kashiwara, Osaka|en}}, [[Osaka Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Anfukuji Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kashiwara, Osaka) - 32 tombs
=== Chūgoku Region === * {{ill|Jūōmen Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Matsue|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Shimane Prefecture]]) - 37 tombs * {{ill|Kitsunedani Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} (Matsue, Shimane) - 16 tombs * {{ill|Kamikonishi Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Izumo, Shimane|en}}, Shimane) * {{ill|Takatsuku Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Chibu, Shimane|en}}, [[Oki District]])
=== Kyushu Region === * {{ill|Takenami Site|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Yukuhashi|en}}, [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]) - Over 1,000 tombs * {{ill|Shiroyama Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Fukuchi, Fukuoka|en}}, Fukuoka) - National Historic Site, over 200 tombs * {{ill|Mizumachi Site|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Nōgata|en}}, Fukuoka) - Prefectural Historic Site, over 40 tombs * {{ill|Kozuki Cave Tomb|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Kurate, Fukuoka|en}}, Fukuoka) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Nagino Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Tamana|en}}, [[Kumamoto Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Ishinuki Anakanon Cave Tomb Cluster|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tamana, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site * {{ill|Nabeta Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Yamaga, Kumamoto|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 60 tombs * {{ill|Ōmura Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Hitoyoshi|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Kumamoto) - National Historic Site, 27 tombs * {{ill|Yokkaichi Cave Tomb Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Usa|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Ōita Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site, 161 tombs * {{ill|Takio Hyakka Cave Tomb Kofun Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Ōita, Ōita|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Ōita) - Municipal Historic Site, 75 tombs * {{ill|Hasugaike Kofun Cluster|en}} ({{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]) - National Historic Site

'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.

The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.

The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Sendai City Board of Education, ‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’ (Sendai City Cultural Property Survey Report No. 9), March 1976.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ''' [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000065.html Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group]

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Sendai City Board of Education, ‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’ (Sendai City Cultural Property Survey Report No. 9), March 1976.
== External Links == * [http://www.city.sendai.jp/Category/bunka/02.html Cultural Properties of Sendai] (Sendai City Board of Education) ''' [http://www.city.sendai.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/iseki/c0000000065.html Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group]

revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.

After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atagoyama Cave Tombs|qq=}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|qq=}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|qq=}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainenji-san|qq=}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōzenji|qq=}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|qq=}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Koizumi Site|qq=}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōzenji (Sendai)|qq=}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|qq=}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Riverside Road|qq=}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Anfukuji Cave Tomb Cluster]] → [[Anfuku-ji (Kashiwara)]]
revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atagoyama Cave Tombs|qq=}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|qq=}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|qq=}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainenji-san|qq=}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōzenji|qq=}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|qq=}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Koizumi Site|qq=}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōzenji (Sendai)|qq=}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|qq=}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Riverside Road|qq=}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Cave tombs]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group''' (宗禅寺横穴墓群) is a group of [[rock-cut tombs]] located in [[Negishimachi, Taihaku Ward, Sendai City, Miyagi Prefecture]]. It is also called '''Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group'''. These tombs were constructed on the right (southwest) bank of the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]] from the late 7th century to the late 8th century. While the total number of tombs is unknown, 15 have been discovered.
== Location == The area where the [[Hirose River (Miyagi)|Hirose River]]'s right bank transitions from hills to flatland contains other tomb groups, such as the {{ill|Atagoyama Cave Tombs|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|ja|愛宕山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Atagoyama Cave Tombs|qq=}} and {{ill|Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|大年寺山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainenji-san Cave Tomb Tomb Group|qq=}}. Along with the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group, these form the {{ill|Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|ja|向山横穴墓群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukaiyama Cave Tomb Tomb Group|qq=}}. The Sōzenji Cave Tomb Tomb Group is situated where the ridge of {{ill|Dainenji-san|ja|大年寺山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainenji-san|qq=}} extends and nearly touches the Hirose River, within the cemetery of {{ill|Sōzenji|ja|宗禅寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōzenji|qq=}}. Today, there is a flat [[terrace (geology)|terrace]] about 10 meters above the river, but in ancient times, the ridge extended further, forming a small valley.
The tombs of the Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group were created by digging horizontally into the slopes of this valley. There are 15 confirmed rock-cut tombs arranged in an east-west direction. Over time, sediment filled the valley, leaving behind a 2-3 meter cliff, burying most of the tomb entrances except for a few. Excavations did not extend deeply into the buried valley, so additional undiscovered tombs may still exist <ref name=hokokusho8p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.8.</ref>.
To the southeast of the site lies the {{ill|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|ja|郡山遺跡|Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins=Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|lt=Koriyama Site|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sendai Kōriyama Kanga ruins|qq=}}, believed to have housed the capital of [[Mutsu Province]] until 724. This coincides with the period when the rock-cut tombs were in use. On the opposite side of the Hirose River to the east is the {{ill|Minami-Koizumi Site|ja|南小泉遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Koizumi Site|qq=}}, a settlement that has existed since the [[Kofun period]]. These settlements are considered potential residences of the tomb users.
== Overview of the Rock-Cut Tombs == The excavators numbered the 15 rock-cut tombs sequentially from west to east, with tomb 1 being closer to the mountain and tomb 15 closer to the river. The first tomb discovered in [[1953]] (Shōwa 28) was tomb 15. All tombs extend perpendicularly into the cliff, aligning with the natural contours of the terrain. At the western end, they face south, while at the eastern end, they face southeast. With the exception of tombs 7, 8, and 9, they are at approximately the same elevation.
Each tomb has a [[burial chamber]] (玄室), which is either square, [[rectangle|rectangular]], or [[trapezoidal]]. The floor of these chambers ranges from 1.4 to 2.75 meters in length. The chamber ceilings are either arched or resemble house roofs. The entrance (玄門) is narrower than the access tunnel ([[Sendou|羨道]]). Notably, tomb 13 lacks a distinct entrance or tunnel, with a uniform width of 0.8 meters. Tombs 11 and 12 have adjacent burial chambers connected by a small hole. Tombs 1, 2, and 3 also share linked chambers, but this appears to be a later modification using a [[pickaxe]]-like tool.
Seven of the tombs feature raised platforms about 10 cm high, except for tomb 15, which has a prominent 50 cm-high platform. Eight tombs lack these platforms. Natural fractures in the rock allowed water to seep in, so some tombs include drainage channels. Human remains were found on the platforms in tombs 5 (one individual) and 9 (three individuals). Due to significant deterioration, only a few large bones, including skulls, were recovered. The remains in tomb 5 belonged to a middle-aged man, while those in tomb 9 included two middle-aged men and one young adult woman.
Artifacts found in seven tombs include 13 pieces of [[sue ware|須恵器]], 8 pieces of [[haji ware|土師器]], and a single iron [[knife|刀子]]. These artifacts were discovered in three locations: inside the burial chambers, outside the entrance closures, and in the forecourts. This suggests they served different purposes—burial goods placed with the deceased, offerings made after the funeral, and items prepared for later interments. The knife, about 15.6 cm long with a wooden handle, was found on the platform in tomb 5, near the human remains.
== Later History == Long after the tombs were abandoned, {{ill|Sōzenji (Sendai)|ja|宗禅寺 (仙台市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōzenji (Sendai)|qq=}} was founded slightly north of the site during the medieval period. The cliff where the tombs are located became part of the temple grounds, with both the top and base of the cliff serving as cemetery areas <ref name=hokokusho4p>‘Sendai City Negishimachi Sōzenji Cave Tomb Group Excavation Report’, p.4.</ref>. In tomb 4, a later grave was dug so close to the tomb ceiling that a 30 cm hole had already opened by the time of excavation <ref name=hokokusho8p></ref>.
The tombs were well known to locals and the temple, and at times, vagrants took shelter inside. At an unknown date, three tombs were connected internally, likely by residents expanding the space. During the early [[Shōwa period]], local residents sealed the tomb entrances with soil for safety reasons <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>.
In [[1953]], during construction of a road along the Hirose River near the {{ill|Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|ja|宮沢橋 (広瀬川)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazawa Bridge (Hirose River)|qq=}}, excavation work uncovered one of the tombs <ref name=hokokusho4p></ref>. In the [[1970s]], when {{ill|Hirose Riverside Road|ja|広瀬河畔通り|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirose Riverside Road|qq=}} was planned, the temple cemetery had to be relocated, and the cliff containing the tombs was included in the new cemetery site. From January 13 to February 15, [[1975]] (Shōwa 50), the Sendai City Board of Education and the Civil Engineering Department conducted a 300m² excavation, confirming and investigating 14 tombs. After the survey, parts of the site were destroyed for leveling, and the remaining tombs were buried. Today, the site lies beneath the Sōzenji cemetery, with no visible traces of the tombs.
== References == {{reflist}}
